v.t: Emperor Cheng of Han changes era to Hongjia
v.s: Emperor Cheng of Han changed the era name to "Hongjia", meaning "Great Fortune". After the era change, Emperor Cheng became even more dissolute, building many palaces for entertainment at enormous cost, emptying the treasury.

v.t: Rome completes the conquest of Hispania
v.s: The Roman army completely conquered the Cantabri and Astures of northern Hispania, and the Iberian Peninsula was fully incorporated into the Roman domain. Hispania became an important province of the Roman Empire, providing abundant mineral resources and soldiers.
Rome's greatest poet, Virgil, died in Brundisium at the age of 51. His unfinished epic, the Aeneid, was compiled and published by his friends, becoming the pinnacle of Latin literature.

v.t: Emperor Cheng of Han indulges in pleasure
v.s: Emperor Cheng neglected state affairs, spending all day in pleasure with Zhao Feiyan and her sister. He often left the palace incognito to play in the outskirts of Chang'an, wasting huge sums of money. The court was completely controlled by the Wang clan of consort relatives.

Augustus promulgated the Lex Julia, aimed at encouraging marriage and childbirth while punishing adultery and celibacy. The law required citizens of marriageable age to marry or face tax penalties. These laws reflected Augustus's emphasis on traditional Roman morality.

v.t: Herod rebuilds the Temple of Jerusalem
v.s: Herod the Great began a large-scale reconstruction of the Temple of Jerusalem. He expanded the Temple Mount and built a magnificent temple complex, making it one of the most spectacular religious buildings of the ancient world. The reconstruction project lasted for decades.

In 18 BC, Zhao Feiyan, seeking to gain favor, conspired to falsely accuse Empress Xu of using witchcraft in the harem to curse the emperor. Emperor Cheng sent people to search the harem, found the doll Zhao had planted, and deposed Empress Xu. Soon Zhao Feiyan was made Empress, and the Zhao sisters monopolized imperial favor for over a decade.

Augustus held a grand Secular Games (Ludi Saeculares) celebration, commemorating Rome's entry into a new era of peace and prosperity. The celebration included theatrical performances, athletic contests, and religious ceremonies lasting several days.

Agrippa built the first Pantheon in Rome, dedicated to all Roman gods. The building featured a rotunda and portico design and was a masterpiece of Roman architectural history. The later Pantheon was rebuilt in 118 AD.

v.t: Western Han opens channels of remonstrance
v.s: Facing increasingly severe social crises, Emperor Cheng issued an edict opening channels of remonstrance, encouraging all officials to speak frankly. However, the emperor did not adopt any substantive suggestions, and the reform measures fizzled out.

After successfully slandering Empress Xu, Zhao Feiyan was formally made Empress by Emperor Cheng. Her sister Zhao Hede was made a consort. The Zhao sisters monopolized imperial favor, pushed out other concubines, and left Emperor Cheng without heirs.

The conflict between the Roman Empire and the Germanic tribes escalated. Augustus sent Tiberius to lead an army into Germania, beginning Rome's war of conquest against the Germans. Rome attempted to make Germania a province, but ultimately failed.

v.t: Emperor Cheng of Han changes era to Yongshi
v.s: Emperor Cheng changed the era name to "Yongshi". After the change, Emperor Cheng became even more dissolute, the Zhao sisters seized power, and the Wang consort clan continued to control the court. The decline of the Western Han became irreversible.

The Germanic tribes of the Sicambri, Cherusci, and others united to resist Roman conquest. Tiberius led forces to suppress them, but the Germanic guerrilla tactics put the Roman legions in a difficult position.
Augustus established southern Gaul as the province of Gallia Narbonensis, under direct Roman jurisdiction. This region became the most Romanized area of Gaul.

v.t: Western Han suffers successive years of famine
v.s: The eastern region suffered successive years of floods and droughts, crops failed, and countless people starved to death. Although the court issued edicts for disaster relief, corrupt officials siphoned off most of the relief supplies, leaving the people in dire straits.

Augustus carried out currency reform, unifying the monetary system of the Roman Empire and standardizing the purity and exchange ratios of gold, silver, and bronze coins. The aureus became the standard currency of the Roman world, promoting trade and economic development.

Tiberius led an expedition against the tribes of Pannonia (in present-day Hungary) and conquered them after several years of war. Pannonia became a province of the Roman Empire, providing important military manpower and mineral resources.

v.t: Western Han iron office workers revolt
v.s: Iron office workers (artisans of the government-run ironworks) in Yingchuan Commandery, unable to bear the oppression, launched a revolt under the leadership of Shentu Sheng. The rebel force grew to several hundred before being suppressed. This was one of the frequent refugee uprisings in the late Western Han.

Augustus ordered the construction of the Ara Pacis in Rome to celebrate Rome's military victories in Hispania and Gaul and the arrival of the Pax Romana. This altar is famous for its exquisite reliefs and is now an important ancient monument in Rome.
Augustus sent Agrippa to tour the eastern provinces, resolve local administrative issues, and oversee Herod's reconstruction projects. Agrippa's tour strengthened Rome's control over the East.

v.t: Emperor Cheng of Han dismisses Wang Shang
v.s: Emperor Cheng, believing false accusations from Wang Gen and other consort relatives, issued an edict dismissing Chancellor Wang Shang. Wang Shang died of vomiting blood within three days of his dismissal. Thereafter, the chancellorship was controlled by consort clan forces, and the central government was completely dominated by the Wang family. The emperor acquiesced, and the Western Han dynasty accelerated toward decline.

After Agrippa's death, Augustus ordered Tiberius to divorce his wife and marry his daughter Julia. Tiberius was forced to comply, but the marriage with Julia was unhappy, and Tiberius later retired to Rhodes.

Augustus' most loyal assistant and Rome's most outstanding general, Agrippa, died. Agrippa had commanded the Battle of Actium and built the Pantheon; he was the pillar of Augustus' rule. His death was a great blow to Augustus.

v.t: Emperor Cheng of Han changes era to Yuanyan
v.s: Emperor Cheng changed the era name to "Yuanyan" and granted a general amnesty. By this time, the Wang consort clan had become an uncontrollable force, with Wang Gen, Wang Li, and others controlling the court. The emperor continued to ignore state affairs and indulge in pleasure, as the treasury grew increasingly empty and social contradictions deepened.

The Roman Empire established a military frontier along the Rhine, building forts and roads to defend against Germanic tribal incursions. This frontier became the boundary between the Roman Empire and Germania.

Tiberius led an expedition against the rebellious tribes of Illyricum (in the western Balkans) and conquered them after several years of war. Illyricum became an important province of the Roman Empire.

During the reign of Emperor Cheng, due to the dominance of consort clans and the emperor's neglect of state affairs, official corruption became severe. Local officials embezzled bribes and oppressed the people, and social contradictions grew increasingly acute.

v.t: Augustus founds new cities in Gaul
v.s: Augustus established several Roman-style cities in Gaul, such as Lugdunum (Lyon) and Augusta Treverorum (Trier), promoting Roman culture and the Latin language. These cities became centers of Romanization in Gaul.

After the death of Parthian king Phraates IV, his son Phraates V came into conflict with the nobility, leading to civil war. Parthia was temporarily unable to attend to eastern affairs, providing space for the rise of the Kushan Empire.

v.t: Emperor Cheng of Han travels incognito
v.s: Emperor Cheng often left the palace incognito to play in the outskirts of Chang'an and even sought out beautiful women among the people. His dissolute behavior aroused strong discontent among court officials, but no one dared to remonstrate.

After years of campaigning, Tiberius finally conquered the tribes of Pannonia (present-day Hungary). Pannonia became an important province of the Roman Empire, providing abundant mineral resources and military manpower.

The Ara Pacis (Altar of Peace), commissioned by Augustus, was completed in Rome. This altar, with its exquisite reliefs depicting mythological and real scenes of the Roman peace, is a masterpiece of Roman art.

Augustus reformed the Senate, reducing its membership and requiring senators to possess a certain amount of property and undergo moral scrutiny. The Senate became an advisory body under Augustus' rule.

v.t: Emperor Cheng of Han changes era to Suihe
v.s: Emperor Cheng changed the era name to "Suihe", hoping to pacify the realm. However, by this time the Western Han society was riddled with crises: land annexation was severe, and the refugee problem was acute. The emperor remained indulging himself in the harem, while Wang Mang and other consort relatives held power. This era change could not reverse the decline of the Western Han.

Wang Mang, known for his humility, frugality, and respect for worthy men, was appointed Grand Marshal by Emperor Cheng and began to grasp power over the court. Wang Mang later usurped the throne and established the Xin dynasty.
The famous Roman poet Horace died in Rome at the age of 57. His works, including the Odes, Satires, and Ars Poetica, had a profound influence on Western poetry and literary criticism.

v.t: Emperor Cheng of Han dies suddenly, Emperor Ai of Han ascends
v.s: Emperor Cheng (Liu Ao) died suddenly in the Weiyang Palace, reportedly from a stroke caused by excessive indulgence. Crown Prince Liu Xin ascended the throne as Emperor Ai. Emperor Cheng reigned for 26 years, during which the Wang consort clan monopolized power and the Western Han's strength severely declined.

Tiberius, due to his deteriorating relationship with his wife Julia and dissatisfaction with Augustus' succession arrangements, voluntarily retired to Rhodes to live in seclusion. He later returned to Rome at Augustus' request.

Liu Xin continued his father Liu Xiang's work of collating the imperial library, compiling the "Seven Summaries", China's first classified book catalog. Liu Xin was also an important representative of the Old Text Confucianism school.

v.t: Emperor Ai of Han restrains the consort clans
v.s: After ascending the throne, Emperor Ai attempted to curb the power of the Wang consort clan and promoted the families of his grandmother Empress Dowager Fu and his mother Empress Dowager Ding. He dismissed Wang Mang from his post as Grand Marshal and appointed his own confidants.

Augustus' grandsons Gaius and Lucius (born to Agrippa and Julia) grew up and were designated by Augustus as his heirs. Augustus personally taught them, cultivating their political and military abilities.

Augustus established an imperial mint in Rome, unifying coinage standards. The mint produced gold, silver, and bronze coins bearing Augustus' portrait and propaganda inscriptions, strengthening the image of imperial authority.

v.t: Emperor Ai of Han favors Dong Xian
v.s: Emperor Ai favored the handsome Dong Xian, sharing his bed and granting him enormous wealth. Dong Xian rose to the position of Grand Marshal, wielding power over the court. The emperor even considered passing the throne to Dong Xian, arousing strong resentment among court officials.

v.t: Emperor Ai of Han changes era to Jianping
v.s: Emperor Ai changed the era name to "Jianping" in an attempt to save the declining Western Han. After ascending the throne, he tried to curb the consort clans and promote his grandmother's Fu family. However, Emperor Ai was weak and ill, and he doted on his male favorite Dong Xian, so the court remained chaotic as before. Landannexation and refugee problems continued to worsen.

Augustus' grandson Gaius Caesar, having turned 15, was granted the title "Prince of Youth" and began to participate in politics. Augustus hoped he would inherit the throne, but Gaius was later wounded in the East and died.

Augustus consolidated the Roman Empire's frontiers, establishing a defensive system along the Rhine, Danube, and Euphrates rivers. He abandoned plans to conquer all of Germania and adopted a defensive posture.

During the reign of Emperor Ai, the families of Empress Dowager Fu and Empress Dowager Ding, the Wang consort clan, and Dong Xian all struggled for power, creating utter chaos in the court. Peasant uprisings and refugee problems grew increasingly severe, and the Western Han was on the verge of collapse.

After Herod the Great's death, his sons fought for the throne, and Rome intervened to mediate. Augustus divided Herod's kingdom among his three sons: Archelaus, Herod Antipas, and Philip. Judea fell into turmoil.

Herod the Great, king of Judea, died at Jericho at about age 69. He was a cruel but capable ruler who built the Masada fortress, the port of Caesarea, and rebuilt the Temple of Jerusalem. His death marked the end of the Herodian dynasty's golden age.

v.t: Gaius Caesar sent to the East
v.s: Augustus sent his grandson Gaius Caesar to the East to handle matters concerning Armenia and Parthia. Gaius Caesar's eastern mission demonstrated Augustus' grooming of his heir.

v.t: Emperor Ai of Han fears natural disasters
v.s: In the 4th year of Jianping (3 BC), the eastern region suffered successive severe droughts and locust plagues, with total crop failure and starving people everywhere. Emperor Ai, fearful, issued an edict taking blame and granting a general amnesty. However, due to financial difficulties and theexpansion of the Wang consort clan, the government failed to take effective relief measures.

v.t: Emperor Ai of Han changes era to Yuanshou
v.s: In 2 BC, Emperor Ai changed the era name to "Yuanshou" (meaning Long Life) and granted a general amnesty. By this time, the Western Han was in a precarious state: landannexation was severe, refugees were everywhere, and the Wang consort clan's power wasincreasinglyexpansion. Emperor Ai, weak and ill with no heirs, was unable to effectively govern. He placed his hopes on the era change for a turnaround, but social contradictions had become intractable. In the 2nd year of Yuanshou (1 BC), Emperor Ai died at only 26 years old.

In the 1st year of Yuanshou (2 BC), Emperor Ai enfeoffed his favorite Dong Xian as Marquis of Gao'an with a fief of 2,000 households, and later promoted him to Grand Marshal. Dong Xian, only 22 years old, wielded power over the court and ranked among the Three Excellencies. The emperor even declared his intention to abdicate in favor of Dong Xian, which, though not carried out, reflected the political absurdity of the late Western Han.

Gaius Caesar met with Parthian king Phraates V on the banks of the Euphrates, where both sides confirmed Armenia's status as a Roman protectorate. This meeting strengthened Rome's position in the East.
Augustus was again chosen by the Senate as Princeps Senatus (First Man of the Senate), a position that allowed him to speak first in the Senate, further consolidating his power.

v.t: Emperor Ai of Han dies, Emperor Ping of Han ascends
v.s: Emperor Ai (Liu Xin) died in the Weiyang Palace at the age of 25. Having no sons, Wang Mang returned to power, installing the 9-year-old Liu Jizi, Prince of Zhongshan, as Emperor Ping of Han. Wang Mang appointed himself Grand Marshal and monopolized court affairs. The Western Han existed in name only.

After Emperor Ai's death, Wang Mang, with the support of Wang Zhengjun, returned to serve as Grand Marshal, taking charge of all court affairs. He eliminated the Fu and Ding consort clan forces from Emperor Ai's reign, demoted Dong Xian, and began preparing for his usurpation of the throne.

v.t: Gaius Caesar wounded in the East
v.s: Gaius Caesar was severely wounded while besieging an Armenian city. He was forced to return to Rome but died on the way. Augustus lost his most favored heir.

The Greek historian Dionysius of Halicarnassus completed his magnum opus, "Roman Antiquities", in 20 volumes, providing a detailed account of Roman history from mythological times to the First Punic War. This work provided important material for later scholars studying early Roman history.
